questions.question: Explain the three main components of a computer system and their functions.
keyPoints: Central Processing Unit (CPU) - executes instructions and controls system operation
Main Memory (RAM) - temporarily stores data and instructions for immediate access
Input/Output Devices - enable user interaction and data exchange with external world

questions.question: Describe the instruction execution cycle with its four phases.
keyPoints: Fetch - retrieve instruction from memory using address in Program Counter
Decode - interpret the instruction to determine operation and operands
Execute - perform the operation in ALU or move data between registers
Store - write results back to memory or registers for future use

questions.question: What is the system bus? Explain the three types of system buses.
keyPoints: System bus is a communication pathway connecting CPU, memory, and I/O devices
Data Bus - carries actual data between components (bidirectional)
Address Bus - carries memory addresses from CPU to specify locations (unidirectional)
Control Bus - carries control signals like read/write, interrupts (timing and control)

questions.question: Explain the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory with examples.
keyPoints: Volatile memory loses stored data when power is removed
RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile - used for temporary storage
Non-volatile memory retains data without power
ROM, Hard Disk, SSD, Flash Memory are non-volatile examples

questions.question: What is an interrupt? Explain its role in computer system operation.
keyPoints: Interrupt is a signal to CPU requesting immediate attention
Suspends current execution to handle urgent event
Types include hardware interrupts (from devices) and software interrupts (from programs)
Allows efficient handling of I/O without continuous CPU polling

questions.question: Explain the memory hierarchy concept and its importance.
keyPoints: Memory hierarchy arranges storage in levels based on speed and size
Top: Registers (fastest, smallest), then Cache, then RAM, then Secondary Storage
Purpose is to balance cost, capacity, and access speed
Uses principle of locality - frequently accessed data kept in faster storage

questions.question: What is DMA? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: DMA (Direct Memory Access) allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory
Without DMA, CPU must handle each data transfer individually
With DMA, device controller transfers data autonomously
CPU is freed to perform other computations during I/O operations
Commonly used by disk drives, network cards, sound cards

questions.question: Draw and explain the block diagram of a computer system showing the relationship between CPU, memory, and I/O devices through the system bus.
keyPoints: Draw CPU box with ALU and Control Unit inside
Show three buses connecting CPU to Memory and I/O
Data bus: bidirectional arrow for actual data transfer
Address bus: unidirectional from CPU to memory/I/O
Control bus: carries read/write signals, interrupt requests, clock
Explain how CPU initiates communication by placing address on address bus
Memory responds to read/write signals on control bus
Data flows on data bus between CPU and selected device

questions.question: Explain the boot process of a computer system from power-on to loading the operating system.
keyPoints: Power Supply initializes and provides stable voltage
BIOS/UEFI stored in ROM performs Power-On Self-Test (POST)
POST checks basic hardware components (memory, keyboard, display)
Bootstrap loader located in ROM is executed
Bootstrap loader reads first sector of boot device (hard disk/SSD)
Bootloader loads operating system kernel into main memory
Operating system initializes device drivers and system services
Control transfers to OS, user interface becomes available
Explain significance of each step for system functionality

questions.question: Explain the differences between RISC and CISC architectures. Also discuss their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) - simple, few instructions, fixed length
CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) - many complex instructions, variable length
RISC advantages: simpler design, faster execution per instruction, pipelining easier
CISC advantages: fewer instructions per program, more powerful instructions
RISC disadvantages: requires more instructions for complex tasks, more memory needed
CISC disadvantages: complex CPU design, variable execution time, harder to pipeline
Modern processors often use hybrid approaches
Examples: RISC - ARM, MIPS; CISC - x86, x64

questions.question: Explain the role of the operating system as an intermediary between hardware and application software.
keyPoints: OS abstracts hardware complexity from applications and users
Resource Management: allocates CPU time, memory, I/O bandwidth to processes
Process Management: creates, schedules, and terminates processes
Memory Management: tracks memory allocation, implements virtual memory
File System: provides organized storage and retrieval of data
Device Management: uses drivers to communicate with hardware devices
Security and Protection: prevents unauthorized access to resources
User Interface: provides CLI or GUI for user interaction
Examples: Windows, Linux, macOS, Android
